name: Salt Valley
short_description: BLM rockhounding near Moab featuring colorful agates and jasper in the Salt Valley anticline. Free public access with scenic views of the Arches region.
description:
Salt Valley is a classic Utah rockhounding destination on public BLM land near Moab, located within the iconic Salt Valley anticline geological structure. The valley's colorful exposures yield banded agates, red and brown jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens that range from thumb-sized to fist-sized pieces. The landscape is high desert with scattered piñon and juniper, offering excellent visibility for spotting gemstone material weathering out of the red Entrada Sandstone and overlying formations. This is a beginner-friendly site that requires minimal hiking and allows free collection within federal regulations.

The geology here is dominated by the Salt Valley anticline, a massive fold in the earth's crust created by subsurface salt movement during the Tertiary period. Silica-rich fluids percolating through fractured sandstone deposited agate and chalcedony nodules and seams over millions of years. The colorful banding in the agates reflects variations in mineral content and depositional conditions. Weathering and erosion have exposed these gemstones at the surface, making them accessible to rockhounders.

Visitors should focus their search along wash bottoms, canyon walls, and exposed ridges where erosion has concentrated loose material. The red soil and rock make colorful agates stand out visually. Water-worn specimens are common in drainage areas. This location combines productive rockhounding with stunning views of the Arches National Park landscape, making it a rewarding experience for both beginners and experienced collectors.
history: Salt Valley's geological formation began in the Permian and Triassic periods with the deposition of thick salt beds. During the Tertiary, subsurface salt movement created the dramatic anticline fold visible today. Miocene-age silica-rich fluids migrating through fractured sandstone layers deposited the agate and chalcedony nodules that rockhounders collect now. This region has been known to local prospectors for decades as a productive area for colorful semi-precious gemstones.
tips: Search wash bottoms and erosional gullies first - these low-lying areas concentrate loose gemstone material naturally. Look for color contrast: colorful banded agates and reddish-brown jasper stand out against the tan sandstone. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and 10x hand lens to identify finer specimens. Early morning light makes the gemstone colors more visible and reduces glare off the red rock.
terrain_notes: Salt Valley ranges from 4,800 to 5,400 feet elevation with moderate rolling terrain and occasional gentle slopes. The landscape is high desert scrubland dominated by piñon pine and juniper with sparse grass. Most productive areas are within 1-3 miles of vehicle parking, accessible via short to moderate hikes. Footing is rocky and uneven with scattered loose red rock and sand. No significant elevation gain required for main collecting areas.
best_season: October - April. The high desert heat (90+ degrees F) makes summer collecting uncomfortable, and afternoon thunderstorms in late summer create flash flood risk in washes. Fall and spring offer mild temperatures (50-70 degrees F) and clear skies ideal for all-day rockhounding.
written_directions: From Moab, head north on US 191 for approximately 12 miles. Turn left (west) onto Salt Valley Road (unsigned BLM road, located just north of Arches National Park entrance). Follow the rough dirt road for 4-5 miles into Salt Valley. Multiple pullouts and parking areas are available along the road and at various wash access points. High-clearance vehicles recommended; passenger cars can access lower portions with care. GPS coordinates 38.730493, -109.525055 mark the central valley area.
road_conditions: Rough dirt road, moderate washboard texture, high-clearance recommended but passenger vehicles can navigate lower sections. 4WD recommended after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along Salt Valley Road. No developed parking lot. Park safely off-road in designated turnouts. Capacity varies from 2-6 vehicles per pullout.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or powered digging equipment. Rockhounding is allowed on all exposed surfaces and loose material only - do not damage exposed rock formations or extract material from cliffs. Pack out all trash. Respect private property boundaries at valley edges.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No damage to exposed formations
Pack out all trash
Respect private land boundaries
nearest_services: Moab, 12 miles south. Full services including gas stations, restaurants, grocery stores, and water refill. Visitor center at Arches National Park entrance, 8 miles south.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most hikers in reasonable condition. Unpaved road to main parking areas. Uneven rocky footing in collecting zones. No wheelchair access to productive areas. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Shaded areas limited; sun exposure significant.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Salt Valley near Moab?
faq.answer: Salt Valley BLM land is known for colorful banded agates, red and brown jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. Most pieces range from thumb-sized to fist-sized. The agates often display beautiful striping in reds, browns, whites, and oranges from mineral variations in the Entrada Sandstone formation.

faq.question: Is there a permit required to rockhound at Salt Valley, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Salt Valley is on public BLM land managed under standard recreational rockhounding rules. You can collect free of charge with no paperwork, though you must follow FLPMA regulations: 25 lbs per day maximum, personal use only, no motorized excavation.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Salt Valley?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Summer heat (90+ degrees F) and afternoon thunderstorms with flash flood risk in washes make those months challenging. Fall and spring offer mild temperatures (50-70 degrees F) and clear skies perfect for extended collecting trips.

faq.question: Can families with young children rockhound at Salt Valley?
faq.answer: Yes, Salt Valley is beginner and family-friendly. The terrain is relatively flat with short walks from parking to productive areas. Children ages 6 and up can participate. Dogs are also welcome on leash. The landscape makes colorful gemstones easy to spot even for inexperienced rockhounders.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding at Salt Valley?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-gallon bucket for collecting, work gloves to protect from sharp edges, a 10x hand lens for identifying specimens, and a small pry bar for dislodging nodules. Sunscreen and plenty of water are essential in the high desert. Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support are recommended for the rocky terrain.

faq.question: How far is Salt Valley from Moab and what are the driving conditions?
faq.answer: Salt Valley is 12 miles north of Moab via US 191, then west on rough Salt Valley Road for 4-5 miles. The road is unimproved dirt with moderate washboard and requires high-clearance vehicles. Passenger cars can access lower sections with care. After rain, 4WD is recommended.

meta_title: Salt Valley Rockhounding - Utah BLM Agate Hunting
meta_description: Free rockhounding on BLM land near Moab. Find colorful agates, jasper, and chalcedony. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Perfect for beginners.
